MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares to recent and relevant baselines (DSSM, LexVec, and the prior published best by Salle et al., 2016) and evaluates on the standard ROC Story/Cloze sets using the same metric, which yields a fair head-to-head claim. However, the study omits robustness checks (no hyperparameter reporting, no multiple seeds or significance testing, and no external/held-out validation mentioned) that would strengthen fairness and reproducibility of the SOTA claim. The authors do acknowledge some prior-method caveats (e.g., coefficient interpretation) and related work, but the lack of released code and detailed experimental conditions weakens the ability to fully verify and compare results. | B(4): The paper benchmarks against appropriate and recent baselines (prior deep learning models such as DSSM and the LexVec/Salle et al. 2016 work) on the same ROC Story Cloze data and reports comparable evaluation metrics, even claiming a new state-of-the-art. It fairly acknowledges limitations and discusses when prior methods perform similarly, and analyzes differences via interpretable stylistic features. Minor omissions remain (no mention of code release or hyperparameter details and potential sensitivity of top-coefficient interpretation), so comparisons are strong but not perfectly reproducible/transparently documented.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to recent and relevant baselines (DSSM, LexVec, and the prior published best by Salle et al., 2016) and evaluates on the standard ROC Story/Cloze sets using the same metric, which yields a fair head-to-head claim. However, the study omits robustness checks (no hyperparameter reporting, no multiple seeds or significance testing, and no external/held-out validation mentioned) that would strengthen fairness and reproducibility of the SOTA claim. The authors do acknowledge some prior-method caveats (e.g., coefficient interpretation) and related work, but the lack of released code and detailed experimental conditions weakens the ability to fully verify and compare results.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper benchmarks against appropriate and recent baselines (prior deep learning models such as DSSM and the LexVec/Salle et al. 2016 work) on the same ROC Story Cloze data and reports comparable evaluation metrics, even claiming a new state-of-the-art. It fairly acknowledges limitations and discusses when prior methods perform similarly, and analyzes differences via interpretable stylistic features. Minor omissions remain (no mention of code release or hyperparameter details and potential sensitivity of top-coefficient interpretation), so comparisons are strong but not perfectly reproducible/transparently documented.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the experiments convincingly show that different writing tasks induce measurable stylistic differences and that simple interpretable models perform well, but both flag important reproducibility and robustness gaps (missing hyperparameters, no code, limited sensitivity analyses). Given these substantive methodological omissions and the paper’s occasional overreach in claims, the work is informative but not sufficiently rigorous to merit a higher substance score.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are adequate to demonstrate that different writing tasks induce detectable stylistic differences and that a simple linear classifier can distinguish ending types, and the paper does include some feature analyses (e.g. character vs. word n-grams). However, important methodological rigor is missing for strong causal or generalization claims: no hyperparameters or code are reported, no randomized significance tests or multiple-seed runs are described, and interpretation relies on top-weight inspection despite noting its pitfalls. Given these omissions and limited robustness checks, the work is informative but not comprehensive enough to fully support its stronger claims (e.g., new SOTA and causal interpretation of writing-task effects).

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are strong: simple linear models with interpretable stylistic features convincingly support the claim that writing tasks induce measurable style differences and even yield a new state-of-the-art on the ROC Story Cloze task, and the feature inspections (e.g., character n-grams vs full model) act as useful ablations. Related work is covered and limitations (e.g., caution about interpreting large coefficients and task-induced confounds) are explicitly discussed. However, reproducibility suffers from missing hyperparameter details and no mention of released code, and sensitivity analyses beyond the reported feature subsets are limited, so the study is solid but not exhaustive.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical finding—that simple stylistic features and a linear classifier separate original/right/wrong story endings and improve Story Cloze performance—is plausible and supported by experiments. However, they also concur that key methodological and reproducibility gaps (missing hyperparameters/code, no statistical significance or multiple-seed runs, limited evaluation/generalization and causal controls) undermine the conclusiveness of the causal interpretation; these shortcomings warrant a cautious, mid-level score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents clear experimental evidence that stylistic features allow a linear classifier to distinguish original/right/wrong endings and to beat previously reported numbers on the ROC Story Cloze task, so the main empirical claims are plausible. However, from the provided summary important methodological weaknesses remain: no hyperparameters or code are reported, there are no randomized significance tests or multiple-seed runs, and evaluation appears restricted to the same dataset (raising generalization concerns). The causal claim that different writing tasks produce measurable stylistic differences is suggestive but under-supported without controlled experiments or external validation, and the interpretation of top logistic coefficients is acknowledged as unreliable yet still used for explanation. Given these gaps the results are interesting but not yet conclusive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear claims and supports them with experiments showing that simple style features and a linear classifier can distinguish original/right/wrong endings and even yield a new SOTA on the Story Cloze task; these are plausible given the methodology described. However, the write-up omits important reproducibility details (no code release, no hyperparameter reporting) and I see no mention of statistical significance or additional controls to rule out confounds beyond the already-noted coefficient-interpretability caveat. These are minor but material shortcomings that do not invalidate the main empirical findings, so I rate it a 4.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper’s main contribution is diagnostic and empirical—demonstrating that annotation-writing tasks induce measurable stylistic artifacts—while using standard stylistic features and logistic regression rather than novel methods. Given the clear, practically important finding (style-only models outperforming prior work) and its informative surprise for the field, the contribution is significant though more conceptual than methodological, so a score of 4 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper offers a useful empirical perspective—showing that different annotation writing tasks induce measurable stylistic differences and that a simple linear classifier on stylistic features can exploit this to outperform prior models on the ROC Story Cloze task. However, the methodological tools (logistic regression with character/word n-grams, POS and surface features) are standard, and the core novelty is diagnostic (revealing dataset/annotation artifacts) rather than proposing a new model or formal mechanism. From a theory-oriented standpoint this is an incremental but informative contribution that should have been framed with stronger causal hypotheses or controlled validation to elevate its originality.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper offers a notable and practically important perspective—that differences in annotator writing tasks induce measurable stylistic artifacts—and empirically demonstrates this with simple, interpretable classifiers that outperform prior deep models on the ROC Story Cloze task. The formulation (diagnosing task-induced style confounds) and the strong empirical claim (style-only logistic model achieving new state-of-the-art) are clearly differentiated from prior work, and provide an informative, somewhat surprising result for experts. The techniques themselves are not novel (standard stylistic features + logistic regression), so the contribution is more conceptual and empirical than methodological, meriting a high but not maximal originality score.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and its main contributions are stated early and kept clear throughout (differences in writing tasks; success of a simple style-based classifier). Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent for a primarily empirical, feature-driven study, and the prose quality is judged good. However, presentation could improve by more explicit reporting of experimental details (e.g., hyperparameters/code) and clearer/more informative figures/tables or examples to support feature-inspection claims—omissions that matter for a skeptical, theory-oriented reader assessing reproducibility and interpretability. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (effects of writing tasks, strong performance of a simple style-based classifier). The methods and experiments are clearly described (feature types, logistic regression, dataset splits) and related work coverage is thorough; writing quality is noted as good. Minor presentation issues remain: hyperparameters are not reported and code release is not mentioned, and the discussion of interpreting top logistic coefficients flags reliability concerns but could be clearer about notation/feature frequency effects.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and its main contributions are stated early and kept clear throughout (differences in writing tasks; success of a simple style-based classifier). Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent for a primarily empirical, feature-driven study, and the prose quality is judged good. However, presentation could improve by more explicit reporting of experimental details (e.g., hyperparameters/code) and clearer/more informative figures/tables or examples to support feature-inspection claims—omissions that matter for a skeptical, theory-oriented reader assessing reproducibility and interpretability.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (effects of writing tasks, strong performance of a simple style-based classifier). The methods and experiments are clearly described (feature types, logistic regression, dataset splits) and related work coverage is thorough; writing quality is noted as good. Minor presentation issues remain: hyperparameters are not reported and code release is not mentioned, and the discussion of interpreting top logistic coefficients flags reliability concerns but could be clearer about notation/feature frequency effects.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important, practical problem and provides actionable findings—showing that simple, interpretable features reveal stylistic artifacts and achieve strong performance on the ROC Story Cloze task. However, they also note valid methodological and reproducibility limitations (missing hyperparameters/code, limited causal analysis and robustness checks) that temper the contribution. Given the clear, likely influence on dataset auditing and evaluation practices despite these gaps, a score of 4 reflects meaningful impact without claiming it is fully transformative.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper highlights an important and practical issue—the role of writing-task induced stylistic confounds in the ROC Story Cloze data—and demonstrates that simple style features (notably character n‑grams) yield strong, even SOTA, performance without context. However, from my theory‑oriented perspective the impact is limited by methodological gaps (no hyperparameter reporting or code, no controlled experiments to establish causal mechanisms, and reliance on top‑coefficient inspection despite recognition of its pitfalls), which weaken claims about generalizability and change to practice. The contribution is a useful cautionary result that should prompt dataset design and evaluation changes, but it is not transformative absent stronger robustness checks and mechanistic analysis.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important and practical problem—how annotation/writing tasks induce stylistic artifacts—demonstrating that simple interpretable models (logistic regression with character/word n-grams and surface features) both reveal these artifacts and achieve a new state-of-the-art on the ROC Story Cloze task. From my empirical/interpretability perspective, these results will meaningfully change how researchers evaluate story-cloze style benchmarks and encourage more careful dataset design and diagnostic analyses; the finding that character n-grams alone nearly match full models is particularly actionable. While not fully transformative (limitations in coefficient interpretation and lack of reported code/hyperparameters reduce immediate reproducibility), the work opens clear directions for dataset auditing and lightweight, interpretable baselines that others are likely to adopt.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is well-targeted to the NLP community and delivers valuable, interpretable findings about stylistic artifacts in ROC Story Cloze. However, Reviewer A’s concerns about missing methodological details (hyperparameters, seeds, code release, and robustness checks) justify tempering a perfect appropriateness rating; the work is clearly appropriate but falls short of the highest bar for reproducibility and methodological rigor expected at top venues.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic—stylistic effects of different writing tasks and their impact on the ROC Story Cloze task—is squarely within core NLP/ML venues (writing-style analysis, evaluation methodology, and reading-comprehension datasets). The paper addresses the right audience and is mostly appropriately formulated: simple linear classifiers with stylistic features are a reasonable controlled probe and the paper explicitly highlights dataset confounds that matter to this community. However, methodological presentation falls short of top-tier standards (no reported hyperparameters/seeds, no code release, and no randomized significance/robustness checks), which are important for reproducibility and for the strong empirical claims (new SOTA) the authors make.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit: it provides a focused empirical/interpretability study of a high-profile NLP dataset (ROC Story Cloze), demonstrates strong, reproducible-seeming results (a simple linear stylistic classifier that outperforms prior deep models), and delivers actionable diagnostics about annotation-induced style artifacts that the NLP community should care about. The methods and evaluation (logistic regression with interpretable features, inspection of top coefficients, comparison to prior state-of-the-art) match expectations for a top-tier NLP venue, and the findings are directly relevant to researchers building datasets and models for reading comprehension and style analysis.

main_claims: Different writing tasks (writing an entire story, adding a coherent ending, adding an incoherent ending) produce measurable differences in authors' writing styles.
A simple linear classifier using stylistic features can successfully distinguish among original, right, and wrong story endings without using story context.
A style-based classifier achieves a new state-of-the-art result on the ROC Story Cloze task, outperforming previous deep learning based results.
Character n-grams are among the most effective stylistic features (nearly matching the full model), while word n-grams also capture much of the signal.
_summary.methodology: Train simple linear classifiers (logistic regression) using stylistic features (character n-grams, word n-grams, POS distributions, surface features such as sentence length and punctuation) to distinguish among original, right, and wrong story endings from the ROC story/cloze data; analyze most discriminative features by inspecting highest-magnitude coefficients.
baselines: LexVec (Salle et al., 2016 / Speer et al., 2016)
metrics: Human judgement scores (from Mostafazadeh et al., 2016a)
Percentage-based performance comparisons (reported absolute percentage improvements)
limitations: Reliability of interpreting top logistic regression coefficients is unclear because linear models can assign large coefficients to rare features (Yano et al., 2012).
Different writing tasks used to generate data can confound classification problems, posing a methodological challenge for dataset design.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - writing style analysis / story cloze (machine reading)

reviewer_a.name: Theory-oriented Skeptic
reviewer_a.background: Senior NLP researcher with a PhD in computational linguistics and a track record of work on formal models of language and evaluation methodology.
traits: Penalises empirical papers that make causal or explanatory claims without formal models or mechanistic hypotheses — expects explicit proposed mechanisms or controlled experiments to support such claims.

Demands rigorous baselines and robustness checks: will deduct points if there are no randomized significance tests across multiple seeds, no held-out/external validation, or no explicit checks for confounds (annotator/task artifacts).
Is critical of claims of 'state-of-the-art' when simpler baselines are not fully explored or when comparisons reuse the same dataset/annotations without demonstrating generalization.
Prefers papers that include ablation studies isolating which features drive results; penalises feature-inspection claims that rely only on top-weight inspection without statistical validation.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on methodological rigor and convincing causal interpretation; conservative about accepting empirical claims absent formal or controlled support.

reviewer_b.name: Empirical/Interpretability Enthusiast
reviewer_b.background: Applied NLP researcher with extensive experience building datasets and interpretable models; has published empirical analyses of annotation effects and lightweight models that yield insights into task design.
traits: Rewards clear, reproducible empirical findings that reveal dataset/annotation artifacts or practical failure modes, especially when simple interpretable models expose them.

Favors papers that demonstrate strong empirical performance (e.g., beating prior reported results) even with simple models, viewing such results as valuable signals about task formulation and inductive biases.

Values detailed feature analyses and human-readable insights (e.g., which n-grams or POS patterns matter) and will up-score work that provides actionable takeaways for dataset curation and model design.
Likes when authors release code/data and provide diagnostics that practitioners can apply; will penalise lack of code/data release but less severely than methodological flaws.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic and result-driven: favors well-executed empirical studies that are reproducible and that provide interpretable insights into datasets and models.
